title: Time Out New York names EoE among 100 best animated movies
display_title: Time Out New York names EoE among 100 best animated movies
wikitext:
[]: Time Out New York names EoE among 100 best animated movies             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Apr 16, 2014 2:49 pm ...of all time. At number 65. Keith Uhlich writes a blurb on it, proclaims "I'm so fucked up" the most memorable quote. Incidentally you can thank Wes Anderson for voting it in. Go see Grand Budapest Hotel if you're grateful.

[]:             Quote Post by Ray » Wed Apr 16, 2014 2:52 pm Hallelujah!

[]:             Quote Post by hui43210 » Wed Apr 16, 2014 4:50 pm Wow, I've never seen it on one of these lists before, also really cool to see that Wes Anderson got it in there. .... ps, Isn't the I'm so fucked up line only in the dub?

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Apr 16, 2014 5:18 pm This is pretty excellent, actually. EoE, going out and getting some recognition!

[]:             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Wed Apr 16, 2014 5:54 pm The fact that it was even included in this list at all is awesome in of itself.

[]:             Quote Post by Giji Shinka » Wed Apr 16, 2014 6:08 pm Gz, EoE!

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionFan » Wed Apr 16, 2014 7:41 pm Pinocchio at the top of the list, but with no mention of Carlo Collodi's original text.... pfft!

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Apr 16, 2014 9:51 pm This list of animated movies is weird. Jason and the Argonauts? King Kong? I mean, I know why they’re in this list, but technically they’re still live-action films with animated special effects. EDIT: Scratch that. I don’t know why they’re there. I mean, does that mean Jurassic Park and the LotR movies also get a spot on this list for their animated special effects?

[]:             Quote Post by Clover » Wed Apr 16, 2014 11:29 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: This list of animated movies is weird. Jason and the Argonauts? King Kong? I mean, I know why they’re in this list, but technically they’re still live-action films with animated special effects. EDIT: Scratch that. I don’t know why they’re there. I mean, does that mean Jurassic Park and the LotR movies also get a spot on this list for their animated special effects?

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Apr 16, 2014 11:38 pm It's because those movies are noteworthy specifically for their animation. This is explained in some of the individual top 10 lists.

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionFan » Wed Apr 16, 2014 11:47 pm Apparently their definition of an 'animated' film is loose so that those suggestions by the 'contributors' can appear in the list. Of course Roger Rabbit finds its own spot on these lists, but I agree with FFF4E that King Kong is a rather odd choice. It makes me wonder if they would have considered Pink Floyd: The Wall , as in my opinion it's probably more compelling than Watership Down (which I suspect I saw 'once' way back when I was a youngin') . As for the list itself... I feel it's fairly evident that the 'contributors' are really only drawing from animated films that are readily available in the UK and North America. Or at least, I mean this in regards to the 'anime' films that appear on the list: a fine selection of the Ghibli films that everyone knows, the Satoshi Kon films that critics and fans know, the two Oshii films that everybody knows, and the one Evangelion film that all serious filmfags know is mandatory viewing for anyone who wishes to be regarded as a serious filmfag*. I don't disagree with the presence of those films on the list (though there are a few I have not seen, for instance, Whisper of the Heart ) but the reason I bring it up the 'availability' concern is because there are a few anime films that I and others here would feel ought to be included on any 'legitimate' best animated films list. The first film that came to mind was Do You Remember Love? . I admit isn't one would feel is isn't 'universally' appreciated as some of these other films are (or are the subject of such claims) but at least among those here who have seen it, I am fairly confident there is a consensus that is a fine film that ought to be considered. I also thought of a few other films, or filmmakers - [s]for instance, Mind Game is an amazing animated flick but I don't know if it is available in North America or the UK[/s] [amendment: I did see Mind Game on the list but I guess I forgot it was there], and though I feel The Adolescence of Utena ought to be included on such a list it's understandable why it wouldn't be included on this family-film-dominated list. It's also worth noting the absence of Hosoda and Shinkai - both directors of respected films, but it appears that their films have not accumulated enough critical attention from this group of contributors to be included in the list. On that note, perhaps the individual contributor's lists are more interesting than the aggregate list itself. * 'Filmfag' - an affectionate term used by formerly active user MugwumpHasNoLiver ... 'affectionate' in the "ha ha, you who sit and watch movies for art " kind of way

[]:             Quote Post by ApolloIVtv » Thu Apr 17, 2014 1:00 am I don't know, these guys are putting spirited away and my neighbor totoro over princess monoke, and number 1 is pinocchio? I can honestly say Fantasia or Beauty and the Beast are much better films than that one, when it comes to Disney. I don't know, there are some sections I agree with, some I don't, and it was cool that they put Eva on there, but still, I don't think it was a really big deal.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Thu Apr 17, 2014 1:29 am EvangelionFan wrote: and the one Evangelion film that all serious filmfags know is mandatory viewing for anyone who wishes to be regarded as a serious filmfag*.

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionFan » Thu Apr 17, 2014 1:51 am @gatotsu911: You have appreciate the context of EoE 's inclusion on the list clearer than I have, and have conveyed it very well. Thank you. One note though: do you remember Eva Yojimbo - specifically, remember his interest in introducing Roger Ebert to NGE? As I thought about EoE on the list, I found myself reminded of that - of the internet film geek, and his desire to have his taste(s) recognized and affirmed by the renowned and far-reaching film critic. I can't say he'd - or others - would 'settle' for Wes Anderson, on the contrary, it's probably more interesting to consider how a contemporary writer and director has been influenced by Evangelion than see an critic evaluate it from a vacuum ... of course, it must be said that the 'nerddom' is unlikely to ever have any opportunities to talk with Wes Anderson about Evangelion On Mind Game - see that strikethrough in the middle of one of blocks of texts above? Indeed, it's surprise to see it on the list - and up so high on the list, beforehand I would have certainly thought to see Castle in the Sky and Millennium Actress ranked higher, but nope, turns out there is a strong appreciation for Yuasa's film amongst the contributors... though it could be worthwhile to check again to see who included it in their lists... Oh, and I concur that Mind Game doesn't feel as though it has had a commercial release in the U.S. (at least, not by the major distributors it would seem). It's had a release here: a double-DVD set was released by Madman some years ago, I picked up a copy back in 2011. It's rated 18+ here, if you were curious.

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Thu Apr 17, 2014 3:10 am I'm just happy that there's people that are aware that they know anime films beyond Miyazaki. That's enough for me.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Apr 17, 2014 4:18 am gatotsu911 wrote: This is only really true in the context of internet nerddom, though, as Eva and several of the other movies you mentioned are thoroughly obscure in the Western world beyond the confines of anime subculture. I was genuinely surprised to see EoE on the list, as I've barely ever seen the film even acknowledged by professional film scholars and critics not specifically focusing on anime, and those who do typically react with the befuddlement of an outsider not attuned to the subcultural context within which it's operating - such is the fate of many "cult" films, of which EoE definitely counts as one. I'm convinced that its inclusion is thanks to a handful of voters, like Wes Anderson, who are in on the "cult" and effectively able to proselytize its virtues to outsiders via their standing within more "respectable", mainstream film culture.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Apr 17, 2014 5:53 am I personally think The End of Evangelion is one of the best animated films ever made, and on a technical level should deserve something above 67. (I know it’s better than Pinocchio , but in an industry over-saturated with Americana of some form, what do you expect?) Though, some of that might just be my personal bias. I don’t really have a “Favorite Films” list of any kind since my taste change monthly with whatever kind of visual technique I’m in the mood for. The nice thing about the original NGE series, including Death and Rebirth and The End of Evangelion , is that I don’t have to choose one technique over the other. Whether I’m in the mood for French Impressionism, German Expressionism, French New Wave, Discontinuous Editing, Cognitive Dissonance (through soundtrack), Stream of Consciousness narrative, light omniscient third-person narrative, or even light Film Noir lighting elements (Asuka and Rei’s dialogue scene in the middle of Episode 16), it’s all in there somewhere. In fact, I think the only major film genre technique it’s missing is the Russian Montage.

[]:             Quote Post by hui43210 » Thu Apr 17, 2014 8:39 am YEAAA I have no idea what they were smoking when they picked Pinocchio at #1. Its not even close to the best Disney film let alone best animated film of all time.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Apr 17, 2014 10:25 am Well, as I understand it Pinocchio has the reputation of the best Disney film. I mean, if not Pinocchio what would it be? Snow White or Fantasia, I guess. But I can't think of anything else that competes in status with Pinocchio. Also they don't "pick" the best one by hand, Pinocchio just ended up at the top of the score charts. FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: I personally think The End of Evangelion is one of the best animated films ever made, and on a technical level should deserve something above 67. (I know it’s better than Pinocchio , but in an industry over-saturated with Americana of some form, what do you expect?)

[]:             Quote Post by El Squibbonator » Thu May 22, 2014 11:57 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: This list of animated movies is weird. Jason and the Argonauts? King Kong? I mean, I know why they’re in this list, but technically they’re still live-action films with animated special effects. EDIT: Scratch that. I don’t know why they’re there. I mean, does that mean Jurassic Park and the LotR movies also get a spot on this list for their animated special effects?
